Q: Rebuilt weak Quant basics with TTP, detailed notes, recommended practice tests, and error correction.
On the second prep cycle, focused almost exclusively on Quant and used GMAT Club sectional tests.
Studied multiple solution explanations to learn alternate Quant methods and noted conceptual gaps.
Completed a large volume of official, review guide, GMAT Club, Magoosh, and Experts Global Quant questions over the full prep arc.
On the final attempt, made educated guesses on difficult or time-consuming Quant questions instead of letting them derail the section.
Used a few test-day warm-up questions to get into problem-solving mode.
V: Used the PowerScore CR Bible as part of the long-term prep stack.
Stayed focused after a difficult Quant section so that Verbal could be attempted calmly.
Relied on familiarity with question types and overall test focus rather than adding major new Verbal content late.
DI: Added a dedicated Experts Global DI course after mocks showed DI was pulling down the total score.
Used DI sets to become comfortable with all question types and improve timing.
Practiced with non-adaptive but difficult mocks to build timing and test-taking comfort.
On the final attempt, moved on from a calculation-heavy DI question after several minutes rather than letting it consume the section.
overall: Moved from a 555 mock and a 630 classic attempt to a 715 Focus score across three attempts.
Used prior notes to restart quickly after a long break instead of rebuilding everything from zero.
Took mocks at the same time of day as the planned exam to match test-day conditions.
After a second disappointing official score, avoided more mocks and kept practice lighter for the final 20 days.
On the successful attempt, focused only on the question in front of him and stayed calm despite time crunches.

Q: Diagnosed rusty fundamentals such as factorization, remainders, and quotients through analytics.
Targeted specific weak question types instead of trying to finish every available practice question.
Rewired Quant practice around timing after initially spending seven to ten minutes on single questions.
Found the official Quant section easier than mocks, which helped confidence on test day.
V: For CR, learned to predict the type of answer needed before looking at choices.
Used prediction to eliminate wrong answers faster and avoid getting stuck between tempting options.
For RC, used simple passage maps to understand each paragraph’s purpose.
Relied on passage structure rather than overcomplicating RC review.
DI: Recognized that some DI questions are verbal questions presented with data.
Improved Data Sufficiency as Quant fundamentals became stronger.
Worked on avoiding improper combination of DS statements.
Practiced rare verbal-logic DS question types before test day.
Learned DS patterns and table-analysis sorting shortcuts.
Forced move-on decisions in DI because spending too long on each question damaged the section.
overall: Improved from a 395 diagnostic to 655 despite starting late with work and application deadlines.
Learned GMAT mechanics such as unanswered-question penalties and timing only after the initial diagnostic.
Used focused guidance in the final 15 days to replace scattered study with specific priorities.
Built test stamina gradually through section improvement before full-length tests.
Did not save all practice tests for the end because endurance needed earlier development.

Q: Used OG 2019, Quant Review, and GMAT Club guides to practice weak Quant topics.
Rebuilt rusty elementary skills such as prime factorization, LCM, and GCF.
Spent most prep time on Quant because it was the lowest section despite a math-friendly background.
Used untimed problems for accuracy and efficient methods, then timed problems for pacing.
Used streaks to build accuracy and confidence.
Made finishing every Quant section a priority and used educated guesses rather than leaving blanks.
V: Relied on natural reading and writing strength but still studied CR where intuition was insufficient.
Learned to justify CR answers with evidence and logic, especially for weaken and boldface questions.
Practiced going against instinct when the evidence required it.
DI: Practiced Data Sufficiency specifically until the solving methods clicked.
Let Quant and Verbal mastery raise DI performance organically.
Found work-related data skills helpful for DI question formats.
Used the move of DS into DI as an advantage for Quant pacing.
overall: Prepared about 120 hours over one month while not working during the holiday period.
Used mocks to identify weak areas and targeted question-bank practice between mocks.
Took official mocks 1 and 2 twice and used GMAT Club tests and question-of-the-day emails for consistency.
Built confidence with many mocks until test-taking felt like muscle memory.
Returned to targeted practice and timing rehearsal after a late mock-score dip.

Q: Strategically guessed on two Quant questions rather than letting them consume too much time.
DI: Identified DI score fluctuation as the main driver of overall score volatility.
Recognized MSR as consuming too much time for mediocre results and creating pressure on the rest of DI.
Chose to strategically skip the entire MSR set in later practice tests rather than spend major time mastering it.
Accepted a few guaranteed misses in exchange for protecting accuracy and time on the rest of DI.
Used the strategy to make overall performance more consistent.
overall: Returned to the GMAT after an earlier cold 675 and studied consistently for two months.
Took an official practice exam each weekend during the second month.
Learned that improvement can come from managing a weakness rather than fixing every weakness.
Prioritized maximizing total score over proving mastery of every question type.

Q: Used TTP for one month to rebuild Quant fundamentals through easy, medium, and hard tests.
Worked on overlapping sets, probability, permutations and combinations, and other Quant topics during late-stage tutoring.
Used careful reasoning and focus skills to navigate Quant and reach a top Quant score.
V: Sought personal help after weak Verbal limited the score.
Worked on Critical Reasoning with an emphasis on care, thoroughness, attention, and focus.
Learned that CR skills can support reasoning across the whole exam, not only Verbal.
DI: Worked on graphs and tables and Two-Part Analysis in late-stage tutoring.
Applied improved CR-style reasoning and focus to DI questions.
overall: Persisted through a long path from 450 classic mock to 500 classic official, 575 Focus, GRE attempts, and finally 665 Focus.
Used GMAT Ninja, e-GMAT, TTP, GMAT Club, GregMat, and targeted tutoring at different stages.
Returned to GMAT after trying GRE and realizing the switch was not producing the desired result.
Found that focused personal guidance in the final few weeks helped consolidate attention and reasoning.
Framed the final improvement as a product of persistence, careful reading, and renewed confidence.

Q: Used the Official Guide to learn question types and concepts, then GMAT Club mocks and question banks for practice.
Documented mock performance by topic within each section.
Tracked careless mistakes and addressed them before moving to the next mock.
Experimented with section order and chose Quant first because it was the strongest section.
Used GMAT Club Quant tests as harder-than-official practice while recognizing that real Quant was easier.
V: Placed Verbal after the break because it was the weakest section and needed a reset.
Used the break to calm down after a difficult DI section before starting Verbal.
DI: Expected DI to be difficult and time-consuming compared with mocks.
Used mock review to track DI topic scores and careless mistakes.
Accepted that room for error exists and that every question does not need to be correct for a strong score.
overall: Restarted prep after an inconsistent earlier attempt and adapted to the Focus format.
Analyzed each mock deeply before taking the next one, using weaknesses to decide study priorities.
Experimented with different section orders to find the best sequence.
Preferred extracting maximum value from a smaller number of mocks over taking a large number without analysis.
Used persistence and self-calibration rather than copying another person’s exact strategy.
